Name: sae_hallucination_subspace_suppression
Title: Hallucination-Subspace Suppression via Targeted SAE Feature Steering
Short Hypothesis: There exists a compact cluster of monosemantic SAE features in mid-to-late residual-stream positions that collectively encode 'confident generation without factual grounding,' and causally suppressing this cluster at inference time reduces hallucination rates without proportionally degrading fluency or task performance. Hallucination-prone states are sparse and localizable, not diffusely distributed across all layers.
Related Work: Mechanistic-interpretability work has shown that residual-stream SAE features in LLMs often correspond to identifiable semantic roles, and that activation steering on hand-picked feature directions can shift model behavior. Prior hallucination detection methods rely on dense probing (e.g., linear probes on raw activations) or output-level uncertainty estimates; both ignore the sparse decomposition SAEs provide. Closest precedents: Anthropic's monosemanticity / scaling-laws-for-SAEs work, work on 'knowledge neurons' in feed-forward layers, and TruthfulQA/HaluEval evaluation paradigms. This proposal differs by performing the discriminative analysis in SAE feature space (not raw activation space) and by treating the identified features as causal targets for steering, not just correlational signals.
Abstract: We hypothesize that LLM hallucinations are mediated by a small, identifiable cluster of SAE features that encode 'high-confidence generation decoupled from factual retrieval' in mid-to-late residual-stream positions. We propose to (1) train or obtain an SAE on residual-stream activations of a small open-source LM at a mid-to-late layer, (2) collect paired hallucinated vs. factual completions on a hallucination benchmark, (3) fit a sparse logistic probe in SAE feature space to identify candidate hallucination features, (4) inspect top-activating examples to verify interpretability, and (5) test causal relevance via activation steering, comparing accuracy delta against random-feature steering baselines. Because the resource-intensive original framing (7B-scale model + TruthfulQA) does not fit CPU-only compute, the experiment should be scaled to small models (e.g., GPT-2-small, Pythia-70M / Pythia-160M, or a tiny transformer trained from scratch) and a synthetic or downsampled hallucination-style task (e.g., a fact-recall task with deliberately misleading distractors). A positive result is: SAE-space probe AUC significantly above raw-activation-probe baseline AND steering on identified features causally reduces error rate compared to random-feature steering, on a small but well-controlled benchmark.
Experiments: Experiment 1 (sanity): Load a small pretrained LM (GPT-2-small or Pythia-70M). Cache residual-stream activations at a chosen mid-layer on a few hundred prompts. Train (or load if available) a tiny SAE on those activations. Verify reconstruction error is reasonable and that some features show interpretable top-activating examples.

Experiment 2 (paired data): Construct a small paired dataset of factual vs. hallucinated completions. For CPU feasibility, use a synthetic fact-recall setup: prompts of the form 'The capital of <country> is ___'. Ground-truth answers are scored against a wikipedia/lookup table; model-generated answers are labeled correct/incorrect. Record the SAE feature activation vector at the answer-token position for each prompt.

Experiment 3 (probe): Fit a sparse logistic regression on the SAE feature vectors to discriminate correct vs. incorrect generations. Report probe AUC. Compare to a baseline logistic probe trained directly on raw activations. Hypothesis: SAE-space probe is at least competitive AND yields a smaller number of important features (lower sparsity-of-explanation).

Experiment 4 (interpretability): Take the top-K SAE features identified by the probe. For each, surface its top-activating training examples and inspect by hand (or with claude-cli). Document whether the features are semantically coherent (e.g., 'low-confidence', 'name-of-thing', 'hedging') or are uninterpretable.

Experiment 5 (causal steering): At inference time, subtract / scale the identified feature directions when the model is generating the answer token. Measure change in correct-answer rate vs. a matched random-feature steering baseline. Hypothesis: targeted steering produces a larger correct-rate gain than random steering. Report both effect size and statistical significance via a paired bootstrap.

Experiment 6 (ablation): Vary (a) the layer at which the SAE is trained, (b) the dictionary size of the SAE (small vs. larger), (c) the number of features steered. Look for the dependence pattern that the hypothesis predicts (mid-to-late layers should dominate; smaller dict sizes should already contain the key features).
Risk Factors and Limitations: Compute downsizing risk: small models may not exhibit clear hallucination patterns at the scale the original hypothesis targeted. A negative result on Pythia-70M is consistent with both 'the hypothesis is wrong' and 'the model is too small for the feature to exist'; we cannot fully disambiguate.
SAE quality: training a fresh SAE in this run may produce poorly interpretable features. Where possible, prefer loading an SAE from an existing checkpoint (Pythia SAEs are publicly available).
Synthetic-benchmark validity: a fact-recall task with distractors is not the same as TruthfulQA. Findings may not transfer to open-ended generation hallucinations.
Steering confound: steering may reduce errors by simply increasing refusal/hedging rates rather than improving factual recall. Track refusal rate explicitly.
Compute caps: each BFTS node runs on CPU; per-node training and SAE inference must complete within minutes, not hours. This is the primary practical constraint and may force toy-sized experiments.

Name: sae_epistemic_superposition_entropy
Title: Hallucination Detection via SAE Feature Entropy at Factual-Retrieval Layers
Short Hypothesis: Hallucinations arise when multiple semantically incompatible SAE features are simultaneously co-active at factual-retrieval layers (mid-to-late feed-forward outputs), producing measurably elevated Shannon entropy over the sparse activation distribution. Per-token SAE-feature entropy is therefore an unsupervised, label-free signal for hallucination risk, and may itself be a useful intervention target via feature-concentration steering.
Related Work: Existing hallucination-detection methods rely on supervised probes, retrieval-augmented checks, or output-level uncertainty (entropy of next-token distribution). Mechanistic interpretability work has explored 'knowledge conflicts' inside feed-forward layers but has not, to our knowledge, formulated an SAE-feature-entropy detector. This proposal differs by (a) computing entropy in the sparse SAE-feature space rather than the output distribution, (b) being label-free at inference time, (c) proposing a causal intervention (top-k feature concentration) that follows directly from the detection signal.
Abstract: Output-level uncertainty (e.g., next-token entropy) is a weak hallucination signal: a model can be confidently wrong. We hypothesize that the *internal* SAE-feature distribution carries a stronger, label-free signal: when the model is uncertain about a fact, multiple SAE features encoding competing facts co-activate at the factual-retrieval layer, producing elevated entropy on the sparse activation. We will (1) instrument a small pretrained LM with an SAE at a feed-forward output layer in the mid-network range, (2) compute per-token SAE-feature entropy on a paired hallucinated-vs-factual prompt set, (3) report AUC of entropy as a hallucination detector against a raw-activation-entropy baseline, and (4) test causal relevance via a 'top-k feature concentration' intervention that zeros out all but the top-k features by activation magnitude at high-entropy tokens, measuring whether this reduces error rate. As with the suppression hypothesis, all experiments must be scaled to CPU-feasible small models (GPT-2-small or Pythia variants) and a synthetic fact-recall benchmark.
Experiments: Experiment 1 (setup): Load a small pretrained LM and an SAE on its mid-layer feed-forward output. Cache feature activations on a paired factual / hallucinated prompt set.

Experiment 2 (detector): For each answer-token position, compute SAE-feature entropy H = -sum(p_i log p_i) over normalized activations. Compare entropy distributions for correct vs. incorrect generations; report AUC.

Experiment 3 (baselines): Compare to (a) raw next-token output entropy, (b) raw-activation entropy, (c) supervised linear probe on raw activations. The SAE-entropy detector should beat (a) and (b) and be at least competitive with (c) despite being label-free.

Experiment 4 (intervention): At inference time, identify high-entropy tokens; zero out all but the top-k SAE features by magnitude at those positions. Sweep k. Measure change in error rate vs. a random-zeroing baseline.

Experiment 5 (specificity): Verify high entropy is not just a proxy for 'topic transition' or 'rare word'. Confirm by computing entropy on non-fact-bearing tokens and on legitimate hedging completions.
Experiment 6 (ablation): Vary SAE layer position and dictionary size; report how detector AUC and intervention efficacy depend on layer depth.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Entropy may correlate with 'topic transition' or 'rare token' more than with hallucination per se. Specificity check (Experiment 5) is the key disambiguator.
Top-k feature concentration may *amplify* a confidently-wrong feature in cases where the model has no correct feature active. This is a real failure mode and should be reported, not hidden.
Small-model results may not generalize to frontier models where SAE features are more numerous and possibly more entangled.
Compute caps: same as for Direction 1; experiments must be CPU-feasible per BFTS node.
